title: Chapter 7: Grandma's Voice
content: I wake up and something is wrong.

Not the usual wrong — not the low-grade hum of dread I've been carrying since the contract, not the ambient catastrophe of having a soul that's being deducted from like a paycheck. This is specific. This has a shape.

I was dreaming about her. My grandmother. I know I was, because the feeling of her is still here, warm and close, the way a hand on your shoulder lingers after the hand is gone. But the dream is already dissolving and I'm reaching for it and—
Her voice.
I can't remember her voice.

I sit up in bed. The sheets twist around my legs and my heart is pounding so hard I can feel it in my teeth. That's not a metaphor. My actual teeth are vibrating with my pulse and I'm sitting here in the dark trying to remember what my grandmother sounded like and I can't.

I have the words. "I'm so proud of you, Chloe." I can see them printed in my memory like subtitles at the bottom of a screen. Black text, white background, clean and sterile. I know she said it. I know she said it at my middle school art show, standing next to my terrible watercolor of a cat that looked more like a lumpy potato with whiskers. I know she squeezed my shoulder when she said it.
But the sound is gone.

Not faded. Not dim. Gone. Like someone took an eraser to the audio track and left the captions intact. There should be a voice there. Low and warm, with that slight rasp she got in the mornings. There should be the way she drew out my name — Chlooooeee — like the last syllable was something sweet she was letting dissolve on her tongue. I remember that she did that. I remember it as a fact.
I just can't hear it anymore.

My hands are shaking. I grab my phone off the nightstand and the screen blasts my face with light and I'm blinking, squinting, scrolling through my apps. Voicemails. There have to be voicemails. She called me all the time — silly check-in calls, you-forgot-to-eat calls, I-saw-something-that-made-me-think-of-you calls. She left messages because I was twenty-two and terrible at answering my phone and she never once held it against me.
I find one. Dated fourteen months ago. My thumb hovers over the play button and something in my chest cracks.
I press play.
"Hey, sweetpea, just calling to say I saw your drawing in the — "
The voice that comes through my phone speaker is wrong.

It's her. I can tell it's her. But it sounds compressed, like someone recorded it through a wall, through water, through a filter that stripped out everything that made it real. The warmth is gone. The texture. The specific way she shaped her vowels, the way her breath caught between words. This could be anyone. This could be a robot reading her transcript.
I play it again.
It's worse.
"Hey, sweetpea, just calling to say I saw your drawing in the — "

More of the voice is missing. The words are still there but they're flattening, compressing, like a flower pressed between pages until it's just a brown outline of itself. I play it again. And again. Each time the audio degrades further, losing detail like a photograph copied too many times, and I realize with a sick lurch what's happening.
The file is shrinking. The app is eating it.

I watch the file size drop in my phone's storage — 2.4 MB, 1.8, 1.1 — the Mephisto app running in the background is deleting the audio in real time, scrubbing it from my phone, from the cloud backup, from the server, from everywhere. Every time I hit play, it takes more. The cost of remembering is the memory itself.

I stop playing it. I can't. If I play it again there'll be nothing left and the silence will be permanent and I'm breathing too fast now, hyperventilating into the dark of my apartment, and I need help.
I need someone to help me.

I call the first lawyer I find on Yelp. A real estate attorney in Flatbush. It rings four times and a groggy voice answers and I say, "I need to break a contract with an app," and he laughs. He actually laughs. "Ma'am, it's two in the morning. Delete the app." I tell him I can't. He says, "Settings, general, storage, delete." Like I'm stupid. Like this is a user error. I tell him the app won't let me and he hangs up.

I call three more. I don't care that it's the middle of the night. I don't care that I sound insane. One of them tells me to contact Apple. Another one says, gently, like I'm a child, "You can always make a new account." The third one asks if I'm under the influence of anything and I say, "Yes, a contract that's eating my dead grandmother's voice," and he tells me to call a helpline.
I go to uninstall the app. My fingers are clumsy, shaking, and I find the Mephisto icon and I hold it down and the little X appears and I press it and—
"This application cannot be removed. Contact your administrator."
I press it again.
"This application cannot be removed. Contact your administrator."
I throw the phone at the wall.

It hits the plaster with a crack that should have shattered the screen. It bounces. Lands on my bed. Not a scratch. The phone case is fine. The wall is fine. The Mephisto icon pulses once, gently, like a heartbeat, like it's amused.

I get up. I go to my junk drawer — the one stuffed with old cables and dead batteries and the phone I had before this one, the one with the cracked screen I never bothered to fix. I find it. I hold it in both hands and I bring it down on the edge of the kitchen counter as hard as I can.

The screen splinters. The plastic casing cracks. I do it again. And again. I'm smashing my old phone into pieces on my kitchen counter at three in the morning and the pieces scatter across the linoleum and I'm breathing hard and it feels good, it feels like doing something, and then—

The Mephisto app is on my new phone. The new phone I just bought last month. The phone that never had Mephisto installed on it because I never downloaded it to this device, I never even logged into the same account, I set it up fresh from scratch—
The icon is there. Pulsing. It was already there. It was waiting.

I grab my laptop. I open a terminal — I don't know how to use a terminal, but I'm Googling "how to uninstall app that won't uninstall" and following every tutorial and none of them work because this isn't a software problem, this is something else, something that doesn't follow the rules of operating systems and source code. I'm an art student. I know color theory and composition and how to make a lithograph. I don't know how to fight an app that's alive.
A notification slides down from the top of my laptop screen. The Mephisto logo — the sleek M with the subtle curl at the bottom that looks like a devil's tail if you look too long — and the text:
[Early termination fee: Your life. For further details, refer to Section 89 of your Terms of Service.]
Your life.
Not your data. Not your account. Your life.

I read it three times. I read it until the words stop being words and become just shapes on a screen. I close the laptop. I sit on my kitchen floor and I know I'm not getting out of this. There is no lawyer and no hack and no loophole. I signed a contract and the contract is eating me from the inside out and the early termination fee is the only thing more expensive than staying.
I pick up my phone and I do something stupid.
I call my grandmother's number.

It's been disconnected for two years. She's been dead for two years. Of course it's disconnected. I know this. I know it the way I know the capital of Nebraska — a fact, inert, useless. But my fingers dial the number anyway, the same number I've had memorized since I was eight, and I press call and I expect the three-tone "this number is no longer in service" recording and instead—
It rings.
Once.
Twice.

And then there's static. A wash of white noise, crackling and hissing, the sound of an empty frequency searching for a signal. I press the phone against my ear so hard it hurts and I listen to the static like it's music, like there's something hidden in it, and—
"Chlooooeee."

My whole body locks up. My spine goes rigid and my lungs stop working and I'm standing in my kitchen in the dark and somewhere in the static I heard my name. Drawn out. The 'oe' stretching long and sweet. Her voice. Her actual voice, not the compressed hollow ghost from the voicemail, but the real sound of her, buried in radio noise like a signal from somewhere impossibly far away.

Or maybe I imagined it. Maybe I wanted it so badly that my brain manufactured it from nothing, a phantom limb of sound, my desperate grieving mind weaving a voice out of static the way you see faces in clouds.
"Grandma?" I whisper. The static continues. Unchanged. Nothing else comes through. Just noise. Just the sound of nothing, of absence, of a frequency no one is broadcasting on anymore.
I hang up.

My hands are shaking so badly I almost drop the phone. I slide down the cabinet until I'm sitting on the kitchen floor and I pull the blanket off the back of the couch — I don't remember getting up to get it, but it's around my shoulders now — and I sit here in the dark and I try to cry.

The tears come but they don't work. They slide down my cheeks in a slow, steady line, salt on my lips, and it should feel like something. It should feel like the heaving, gasping, ugly-cry release that always left me emptied out and somehow lighter afterward. The kind of cry where your whole body gets involved, where you sob so hard your ribs ache, where you make sounds that don't seem like they could come from a person. The kind of cry my grandmother used to hold me through, her hand on my back, counting my breaths until they slowed.
This isn't that.

This is a faucet that won't shut off. Just a slow leak. Tears without the mechanism that makes them mean something. The grief deduction hasn't loaded yet and already it's taking — already the feeling of grief is becoming a fact about grief, something I know I'm experiencing without actually experiencing it. I know I should be sobbing. I know I should be wrecked. And I am. But the being wrecked is happening at a distance, like watching myself through a window, like reading about someone else's worst night.

The kitchen floor is cold through the blanket. I can feel the linoleum through the thin cotton, the chill seeping up into my bones, and I think about my grandmother's kitchen. I know it smelled like rosemary and burnt butter — she always burned the butter, every single time, and she'd swear in Italian and start over. I know this as a fact. But I can't smell it. I can't close my eyes and be there, the way I could six months ago, the way I could before the contract. The olfactory memory is gone. It's been replaced by a line item: rosemary, burnt butter, Italian swears. A list of ingredients without the dish.
My phone glows on the floor beside me. I pick it up. The screen is bright and white and it hurts my eyes and I watch the follower count because there's nothing else to watch.
I don't know when it crossed 9 million. I don't remember the notifications. It doesn't matter. What matters is the gap. 156,873 to go. Two weeks, give or take, before I hit ten million.
Before the grief deduction loads.
A notification slides down. The Mephisto logo. Its little demonic curl at the bottom of the M. And the words I've been dreading:
[Grief deduction queued. Estimated load: 14 days.]

Fourteen days. Two weeks. I can't stop it. I can't uninstall the app. I can't break the contract. I can't hire a lawyer or hack the code or smash my way out. The early termination fee is my life. The only exit is death and the only forward is more loss.

I stare at the screen until the Mephisto icon blurs, until the edges soften and the curl at the bottom of the M stops looking like a tail and starts looking like a question mark, and the question is: how much of me will be left when this is over?

My grandmother would know what to say. She'd have some old-country wisdom, some line about endurance or faith or the strength of women who came before. She'd put her hand on my back and count my breaths until they slowed. She'd call me sweetpea and she'd draw out my name like it was the sweetest thing she'd ever tasted.
I need her voice.

And that's the cruelest part, the joke I can't even laugh at: the person I need most is the one whose voice I'll never hear again. Not because she's gone — I was surviving that. But because the app is eating the sound of her from the world, from my phone, from my memory, from the air itself, and soon there'll be nothing left but a transcript without audio, words without warmth, a name without the voice that made it mean something.

I sit on the kitchen floor at four in the morning and the tears keep leaking and the Mephisto icon keeps pulsing and somewhere just out of reach, like a radio station one frequency away from clarity, there's a sound I can almost hear.
Almost.
